xArticle.trendPeg: the next users of SaaS are agents
xArticle.hook: I kept seeing the same advice: make your product agent-ready, then put a trend-pegged article in front of people the moment a release drops. So I built the tool that does both. It's open source. Here's how it works.
xArticle.body:
It's called Riff. You give it one source — a YouTube video, an article, a blog post — and it fans that out into:

- a trend-pegged X article (the headline asset)
- a week of platform-tailored posts
- a real-value SEO blog post
- a split-screen avatar video script

Then it publishes them. Markdown to disk always, plus Postiz, WordPress, and HeyGen when you add keys.

The part I care about most: it's built the agent-native way, in the order that actually works. API, then a CLI, then one skill.md file, then an MCP endpoint. So a human, a Claude Code session, a Cursor user, or someone on ChatGPT can all drive it. The agent does the writing, so there's no token cost to run it.

Two commands make it more than a repurposer:
- `repeg` swaps the trend on top of an already-written article, so you can fire first when a release lands
- `stock` shows your whole stockpile of articles ready to go

Every link it writes is UTM-tracked per channel, so you can see which trend actually converted.

The one rule it won't break: no em-dash spray, no hashtag spam, no invented stats. Every claim has to trace to the source. In a feed full of generated slop, the thing that reads like a real person wins.
xArticle.productMention: Riff is MIT-licensed and runs with zero paid dependencies — export to markdown needs no keys at all.
xArticle.cta: It's free and open source. Install it as an agent skill or just clone it: {{LINK}}

posts.text:
Built an open-source tool that turns one video into a week of content across every channel.

The twist: your AI agent does the writing, not an API you pay per call. Zero token cost to run.

What it does 🧵

One source in:
- YouTube video
- an article
- a blog post

Four things out:
- a trend-pegged X article
- a week of platform posts
- a real-value SEO blog post
- a split-screen video script

Then it publishes them.

It's built API → CLI → skill → MCP, so it works from Claude Code, Cursor, or ChatGPT.

Two commands matter most:
`repeg` swaps the trend on top of a written article so you fire first.
`stock` shows everything ready to go.

The founders who win each model-release cycle aren't the ones with the best model. They're the ones whose product the model can actually use, and who can get a relevant, tracked article in front of the right people the moment a trend lands.

I built an open-source tool around exactly that. Riff takes one source and produces a trend-pegged article, a week of posts, an SEO blog post, and a video script, then publishes them. It's agent-native, so the agent does the writing and there's no per-call cost. Every link is UTM-tracked so you can see which trend actually converted.

It's MIT-licensed. Link in the comments.
Open-sourced a content tool today. One source in, a week of cross-channel content out, your own agent does the writing. No per-call cost. Anti-slop by default.
One video → a week of content across every platform. Your AI agent writes it, you keep the receipts (every link tracked). Free and open source. Link in bio.

blog.title: Riff: Turn One Video Into a Week of Content, Agent-Native and Open Source
blog.metaDescription: Riff is an open-source, agent-native content repurposing engine. One source becomes a trend-pegged X article, a week of posts, an SEO blog post, and a video script.
blog.bodyMarkdown:
## The problem Riff solves

Every model release follows the same curve: a spike of attention, then a fade. The builders who convert that spike are the ones who are already agent-ready when it lands, and who can put a relevant, tracked article in front of the right people fast. Doing that by hand for every channel is slow, and most of the output ends up reading like generated slop.

Riff is an open-source tool that automates the fan-out without the slop. You give it one source and it produces a full multi-channel bundle.

## What it produces

From a single YouTube video, article, or blog post, Riff generates:

- A **trend-pegged X article**, the headline asset, written to ride a current release.
- A **week of platform-tailored posts** across X, LinkedIn, Instagram, and Threads.
- A **real-value SEO blog post** in third person, with the product woven in.
- A **split-screen avatar video script** ready for a tool like HeyGen.

It then publishes them: Markdown to disk with no keys required, plus Postiz, WordPress, and HeyGen when configured.

## Why it's agent-native

Riff is built in the order that works: a public API, a CLI published to npm, a single skill.md file in a GitHub repo, and an MCP endpoint. That means it can be driven from Claude Code, Cursor, or ChatGPT. The buyer's own agent does the writing, so there is no per-call token cost to operate it.

Two commands set it apart from a plain repurposer. The `repeg` command swaps the trend on top of an already-written article so you can fire first when a release drops. The `stock` command shows your entire stockpile of articles ready to go. Every link it writes is UTM-tracked per channel, so you can measure which trend actually drove signups.

## The anti-slop rule

The generation prompt bans em-dash spray, hashtag spam, and invented statistics. Every claim must trace back to the source. In a feed drowning in generated content, the output that reads like a real human operator is the one that wins.

Riff is MIT-licensed and runs with zero paid dependencies. You can install it as an agent skill or clone the repository directly.

It's agent-native. Your own agent does the writing, so there's no per-call cost. And every link is tracked so you know which trend converted.
One rule it won't break: no slop. Every claim traces to the source. It's free and open source.
